spec_match.rationale: Maps the chromestatus 'web applications that will use this information to provide an improved user experience' framing onto the WICG-cited motivating use case (video conferencing) and shows a per-tier preset table (resolution / fps / simulcast / blur / noise suppression).

interactive.rationale: Conference grid mock-up swaps tile count, animation, blur, accent colours, and settings table contents when the tier changes; tier-simulation buttons plus a 'use real reading' button drive the whole stage.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb is a one-liner: 'video conferencing app picks a quality preset from the device's CPU performance tier'. The page delivers exactly that with a representative settings panel and a visible conference UI.

edge_cases.rationale: Origin-trial-missing fallback is in place but no demo of: mid-call tier change (which the spec says should NOT happen, since tier is static), behaviour when the user joins from a tier that can't run the selected blur/NS model, or simulcast layer selection vs unicast fallback.

references.rationale: See-also includes the WICG proposals issue #253 (the conferencing motivating use case explicitly) and https://chromestatus.com/feature/5189864286978048.

design_system.rationale: All colours via CSS variables (including color-mix(in srgb, var(--accent-blue) ...) for the tile faces), tier-btn override pairs background and color, no static-card.

openQuestions.title: Pair with the Compute Pressure API for in-call adaptation
openQuestions.detail: The page correctly notes the tier is static, but real conferencing apps lower bitrate mid-call as the encoder saturates. Add a small note (or a 'show in-call adaptation' toggle) that pairs the static tier ceiling with a Compute Pressure floor — this is the canonical interplay the chromestatus summary calls out.

openQuestions.title: Distinguish simulcast layers from unicast fallback
openQuestions.detail: The settings table mentions 'simulcast layers' but doesn't visually show what swapping from 3 layers to 1 means. Add a small SVG/diagram of the encoded streams (or a layer-count integer with a note) so the visitor sees what changes on the wire.

openQuestions.title: Surface why fingerprinting wasn't the answer
openQuestions.detail: Blurb says 'no proprietary fingerprinting, no benchmark warm-up tax'. Make it concrete — show what the alternative looks like (a 500ms blocking benchmark on join) and the user-visible cost it pays, so the visitor understands the design tradeoff.

summary: Clean rendering of the canonical motivating use case with a real interactive grid. To round it out, pair the static tier with the dynamic pressure signal (mid-call adaptation is the realistic story) and show what fingerprinting alternative cost the user.
